It converts Python bytecode instructions into a symbolic format\\n6: that tracks the flow of tensors and other values through the program.\\n7: \\n8: Key components:\\n9: - InstructionTranslatorBase: Base class for converting bytecode to symbolic execution\\n10: - InstructionTranslator: Main translator for function bytecode\\n11: - InliningInstructionTranslator: Handles inlining of called functions\\n12: - SpeculationLog: Manages state for speculative execution and rollback\\n13: \\n14: The symbolic conversion process handles:\\n15: - Control flow (loops, conditionals, etc.)\\n16: - Function inlining and call stack management\\n17: - Tracking of program values and side effects\\n18: - Graph breaks and resumption points\\n19: - Exception handling and stack frame management\\n20: \\n21: This is a core part of TorchDynamo's tracing system that enables ahead-of-time\\n22: optimization of PyTorch programs.\\n23: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n24: \\n25: from __future__ import annotations\\n26: {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01FqdTPWRvsFgNXZ583jShtZ","type":"tool_result","content":"{\"content\":\"2060:                 context=f\\\"raised exception {curr_exc.python_type_name()}({curr_exc.args})\\\",  # type: ignore[union-attr]\\n2061:                 explanation=observed_exn_gb_explanation,\\n2062:                 hints=[\\n2063:                     *graph_break_hints.USER_ERROR,\\n2064:                     *graph_break_hints.SUPPORTABLE,\\n2065:                 ],\\n2066:             )\\n2067: \\n2068:         if sys.version_info >= (3, 11):\\n2069:             exn_tab_entry = self.current_instruction.exn_tab_entry\\n2070:             if exn_tab_entry:\\n2071:                 # Implementation is based on https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.11/Objects/exception_handling_notes.txt\\n2072: \\n2073:                 # 1) pop values from the stack until it matches the stack depth\\n2074:                 # for the handler\\n2075:                 while len(self.stack) > exn_tab_entry.depth:\\n2076:                     self.pop()\\n2077: \\n2078:                 # 2) if 'lasti' is true, then push the offset that the exception was raised at\\n2079:                 if exn_tab_entry.lasti:\\n2080:                     self.push(\\n2081:                         variables.ConstantVariable(self.current_instruction.offset)\\n2082:                     )\\n2083: \\n2084:                 # 3) push the exception to the stack\\n2085:                 self.push(self.exn_vt_stack.get_current_exception())\\n2086: \\n2087:                 # 4) jump to the handler\\n2088:                 self.jump(exn_tab_entry)  # type: ignore[arg-type]\\n2089:             else:\\n2090:                 # No handler found. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","id":"msg_012Rrnqb7DJSinVR8WNXk6hT","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Now I need to understand the bug more. Let me look at the `generate_based_on_original_code_object` method more carefully, specifically where the KeyError might occur, and understand what needs to be fixed.\n\nLooking at the `generate_based_on_original_code_object` method (lines 573-674 of resume_execution.py):\n\nThe `setup_fn_target_offsets` are remapped using `meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset]`. The code at line 668-671:\n```python\nsetup_fn_target_offsets = tuple(\n    meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][n]\n    for n in setup_fn_target_offsets\n)\n```\n\nThis can fail with a KeyError if `n` (an offset from `setup_fn_target_offsets`) is not in `meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset]`.\n\nThe `remap_block_offsets` function builds `block_target_offset_remap` by adding prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO offsets to old block targets from `meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap`, then finding old bytecode targets by filtering through `old_inst_offsets`. The issue is that `old_inst_offsets` might not contain all the offsets that appear in `setup_fn_target_offsets`, causing the lookup to fail.\n\nI'm realizing the real problem: `setup_fn_target_offsets` are offsets from the resume code, not the original code, so they need to be mapped back to the original bytecode offsets before being used in the remap lookup. I'm realizing the issue: `setup_fn_target_offsets` are offsets from the resume function's code object, not from the original code, so filtering them against `old_start_offset` (which comes from the original code) is comparing offsets from two different code objects. The real problem is that these mismatched offsets are then passed to `ContinueExecutionCache.lookup` along with `meta.code`, creating a mismatch between the offset references and the code object they're supposed to apply to. So `setup_fn_target_offsets` passed to `generate_based_on_original_code_object` are offsets from `code` (the resume code), and `remap_block_offsets` tries to translate them to offsets in `meta.code`.\n\nLet me check what the `block_target_offset_remap` is supposed to store. From the code in `remap_block_offsets`:\n- `block_target_offset_remap[inst.offset] = o` for prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO blocks → maps from resume code offset to original code offset\n- `block_target_offset_remap[old.offset] = new[1].offset` for old bytecode targets → maps from old.offset (resume code offset) to new[1].offset (original code offset)\n\nThen I'm mapping from resume code offsets to original code offsets using the remap table. But there's a potential issue: if `setup_fn_target_offsets` contains an offset from the second-level resume code that falls in the prefix area but isn't part of the PUSH_EXC_INFO blocks, it won't be found in the remap table at all, causing a KeyError. It implements\\n4: bytecode transformation and execution state management to enable:\\n5: \\n6: - Resuming execution at arbitrary points in Python bytecode\\n7: - Managing context managers and their state across execution boundaries\\n8: - Transforming and generating new code objects with preserved execution state\\n9: - Supporting Python 3.11+ exception handling and block management\\n10: - Restoring torch function mode stacks and other execution context\\n11: \\n12: The module is critical for PyTorch Dynamo's ability to optimize code while preserving\\n13: Python semantics and execution state.\\n14: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n15: \\n16: import copy\\n17: import dataclasses\\n18: import sys\\n19: import types\\n20: from collections.abc import Iterable\\n21: from contextlib import \\n573:     @classmethod\\n574:     def generate_based_on_original_code_object(\\n575:         cls,\\n576:         code: types.CodeType,\\n577:         lineno: int,\\n578:         offset: int,\\n579:         setup_fn_target_offsets: tuple[int, ...],\\n580:         *args: Any,\\n581:     ) -> types.CodeType:\\n582:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n583:         This handles the case of generating a resume into code generated\\n584:         to resume something else.  We want to always generate starting\\n585:         from the original code object so that if control flow paths\\n586:         converge we only generated 1 resume function (rather than 2^n\\n587:         resume functions).\\n588:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n589: \\n590:         meta: ResumeFunctionMetadata = ContinueExecutionCache.generated_code_metadata[\\n591:             code\\n592:         ]\\n593:         new_offset = -1\\n594: \\n595:         def find_new_offset(\\n596:             instructions: list[Instruction], code_options: dict[str, Any]\\n597:         ) -> None:\\n598:             nonlocal new_offset\\n599:             (target,) = (i for i in instructions if i.offset == offset)\\n600:             # match the functions starting at the last instruction as we have added a prefix\\n601:             (new_target,) = (\\n602:                 i2\\n603:                 for i1, i2 in zip(reversed(instructions), reversed(meta.instructions))\\n604:                 if i1 is target\\n605:             )\\n606:             assert target.opcode == new_target.opcode\\n607:             assert new_target.offset is not None\\n608:             new_offset = new_target.offset\\n609: \\n610:         transform_code_object(code, find_new_offset)\\n611:         assert new_offset >= 0\\n612: \\n613:         if sys.version_info >= (3, 11):\\n614:             # setup_fn_target_offsets currently contains the target offset of\\n615:             # each setup_fn, based on `code`. When we codegen the resume function\\n616:             # based on the original code object, `meta.code`, the offsets in\\n617:             # setup_fn_target_offsets must be based on `meta.code` instead.\\n618:             if new_offset not in meta.block_target_offset_remap:\\n619:                 block_target_offset_remap = meta.block_target_offset_remap[\\n620:                     new_offset\\n621:                 ] = {}\\n622: \\n623:                 def remap_block_offsets(\\n624:                     instructions: list[Instruction], code_options: dict[str, Any]\\n625:                 ) -> None:\\n626:                     # NOTE: each prefix block generates exactly one PUSH_EXC_INFO,\\n627:                     # so we can tell which block a prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO belongs to,\\n628:                     # by counting. old bytecode targets are after the prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO's\\n647:                     old_start_offset = (\\n648:                         cast(int, prefix_blocks[-1].offset) if prefix_blocks else -1\\n649:                     )\\n650:                     # It implements\n4: bytecode transformation and execution state management to enable:\n5: \n6: - Resuming execution at arbitrary points in Python bytecode\n7: - Managing context managers and their state across execution boundaries\n8: - Transforming and generating new code objects with preserved execution state\n9: - Supporting Python 3.11+ exception handling and block management\n10: - Restoring torch function mode stacks and other execution context\n11: \n12: The module is critical for PyTorch Dynamo's ability to optimize code while preserving\n13: Python semantics and execution state.\n14:  Two-pointer conditional filter.\n    e.g. _filter_iter(insts, sorted_offsets, lambda i, o: i.offset == o)\n    returns the instructions with offsets in sorted_offsets\n    \"\"\"\n    it = iter(l2)\n old_hook_target_remap[old_hook_target] = exn_target\n                if i in stack_ctx_vars_d:\n                    # NOTE: we assume that current stack var is a context manager CLASS!\n  Two-pointer conditional filter.\n    e.g. _filter_iter(insts, sorted_offsets, lambda i, o: i.offset == o)\n    returns the instructions with offsets in sorted_offsets\n    \"\"\"\n    it = iter(l2)\n  Two-pointer conditional filter.\n    e.g. _filter_iter(insts, sorted_offsets, lambda i, o: i.offset == o)\n    returns the instructions with offsets in sorted_offsets\n    \"\"\"\n    it = iter(l2)\n code not in cls.cache:\n            cls.cache[code] = {}\n        key = tuple(key)\n        if key not in cls.cache[code]:\n            cls.cache[code][key] = cls.generate(code, lineno, *key)\n
